message:
Re-serialize beads issues.jsonl with source_repo field normalization

Bulk re-serialization of .beads/issues.jsonl reflecting upstream br sync
changes that added source_repo fields and normalized dependency graph
references across the issue tracker dataset.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -140,6 +140,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-1uy.1.1","title":"Hostcall ABI: Implement Shared Dispatcher (HostCallPayload -> HostResultPayload)","description":"# Goal
Implement the **single source of truth** for hostcall execution:

`extensions::HostCallPayload` (validated) -> capability derivation + policy + timeout -> connector dispatch -> `extensions::HostResultPayload`

# Background
- We currently have multiple hostcall dispatch paths:
  - JS tier: `HostcallRequest` -> `HostcallOutcome`
  - WASM tier: `wasm_host::HostState::call()` bespoke dispatch
  - Protocol v1 types exist (`ExtensionBody::HostCall/HostResult`) but are not routed at runtime.
- This creates divergence risk (security + behavior + logging), and prevents treating hostcalls as an ABI artifact.

# Scope
- Input: `extensions::HostCallPayload` plus execution context:
  - `runtime`: `js|wasm|protocol` (for logs only; never affects authorization)
  - `extension_id` (optional)
  - `tool_registry`, `http_connector`, `fs_connector`, `extension_manager` (session/ui/events), `policy`.
- Output: `extensions::HostResultPayload` that:
  - Always has `output` as an object (`{}` on error)
  - Uses only taxonomy codes: `timeout|denied|io|invalid_request|internal`
  - Optionally emits `chunk` metadata when streaming is enabled later.

# Required Semantics
- Capability derivation is core-defined via `required_capability_for_host_call()`.
- `payload.capability` mismatch is an `invalid_request` error.
- Timeouts:
  - Respect `payload.timeout_ms` when set.
  - Enforce via `asupersync` budget contexts (structured cancellation).
- Policy:
  - `strict|prompt|permissive` behavior must match the existing policy engine.
  - Prompt decisions must integrate with the existing prompt cache (`ExtensionManager.policy_prompt_cache`).
- Logging:
  - Emit `host_call.start` and `host_call.end` and `policy.decision` with:
    - `runtime`, `call_id`, `extension_id`, `capability`, `method`, `params_hash`, `timeout_ms`, `duration_ms`, `error_code`.
  - Never log raw params; log `params_hash` only.

# Method Coverage (v1)
- `tool`: built-in tool registry (+ unknown tool => `invalid_request`)
- `exec`: map to `bash` tool (same sandbox semantics) OR a dedicated exec connector if already present
- `http`: `connectors::http::HttpConnector`
- `fs`: scoped `FsConnector` with manifest scopes
- `env`: scope allowlist from capability manifest
- `session`/`ui`/`events`: route through `ExtensionManager` dispatch helpers
- `log`: structured extension log emission (no ambient stdout)

# Tests
- Unit tests for each method:
  - happy-path
  - denied-by-policy
  - capability mismatch invalid_request
  - timeout mapping

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Dispatcher compiles with no feature flags required for JS tier.
- [ ] All method variants return `HostResultPayload` with correct taxonomy.
- [ ] Deterministic structured logs emitted with params_hash.
- [ ] Unit tests cover success plus each taxonomy branch.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:55:42.245710396Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:55:42.245710396Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","hostcall","protocol"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:55:42.245710396Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1uy.1.2","title":"Hostcall ABI: Wire ExtensionMessage host_call -> host_result (Protocol Adapter)","description":"# Goal
Make the protocol types in `src/extensions.rs` real at runtime by implementing an adapter that:

- accepts `ExtensionMessage(type=host_call)`
- dispatches it via the shared hostcall dispatcher
- emits `ExtensionMessage(type=host_result)`

# Background
- `ExtensionMessage::parse_and_validate()` exists but is not used by any runtime component.
- Having protocol message wiring is useful for:
  - harness/testing (treat hostcall ABI as an artifact)
  - future external runtimes (Tier C process IPC) without re-implementing dispatch logic

# Scope
- Implement a small "protocol adapter" surface (library API), not necessarily a user-facing CLI:
  - `fn handle_extension_message(msg: ExtensionMessage, ctx: ...) -> Vec<ExtensionMessage>`
  - For now, only `host_call` is required; other message types can be `invalid_request`.
- Ensure **streaming-ready** behavior:
  - Return value is a `Vec<ExtensionMessage>` to allow multiple `host_result` chunks later.
  - Initial implementation can emit exactly one `host_result` with `chunk=None`.

# Required Semantics
- The adapter must:
  - preserve `call_id`
  - enforce capability mismatch -> `invalid_request`
  - enforce taxonomy codes
  - never emit raw params in logs

# Tests
- Unit tests:
  - parse+validate `host_call` JSON -> dispatch -> `host_result` JSON validates
  - invalid JSON / invalid version / unknown method -> `error` or `host_result` invalid_request
  - denied-by-policy -> `host_result` denied

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `ExtensionMessage(type=host_call)` can be round-tripped through adapter to `host_result`.
- [ ] All error branches produce valid `HostResultPayload` with taxonomy codes.
- [ ] Adapter is decoupled enough to be used by future process-based extension runners.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:09.534702397Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:09.534702397Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","hostcall","protocol"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:09.534702397Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:09.534702397Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1uy.1.3","title":"Hostcall ABI: JS Runtime Uses Shared Dispatcher + Taxonomy","description":"# Goal
Refactor JS-tier hostcalls (`pi.*` -> `HostcallRequest`) so they execute through the **shared HostCallPayload -> HostResultPayload dispatcher**.

# Background
- Today JS-tier hostcalls flow through `HostcallRequest`/`HostcallOutcome` and return ad-hoc codes like `tool_error`/`SHUTDOWN`.
- The hostcall ABI spec requires **fixed error taxonomy** and shared semantics across JS and WASM.

# Scope
- Create a lossless conversion:
  - `HostcallRequest` -> `extensions::HostCallPayload`:
    - `method` from `request.method()`
    - `params` from `request.params_for_hash()` (or equivalent canonical params shape)
    - `capability` derived (NOT caller-controlled)
    - `timeout_ms` derived from request options where applicable
  - dispatcher output `HostResultPayload` -> JS completion `HostcallOutcome`:
    - success => `HostcallOutcome::Success(output)`
    - error => `HostcallOutcome::Error { code, message }` where `code` is **one of** taxonomy codes only
- Preserve existing deterministic scheduling behavior:
  - completions must still enqueue as macrotasks; no synchronous re-entrancy.

# Required Semantics
- JS hostcall logs must match WASM logs for equivalent calls:
  - `host_call.start/end`, `policy.decision`, `params_hash`, `duration_ms`.
- Remove/translate non-taxonomy codes:
  - `tool_error` -> `io` (or `internal` when clearly a bug)
  - `SHUTDOWN` -> `internal` (or `invalid_request`) but never a new code

# Tests
- Update/add unit tests covering:
  - tool failure maps to taxonomy
  - manager shutdown path maps cleanly (no `SHUTDOWN`)
  - unknown tool => `invalid_request`
  - denied-by-policy => `denied`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] JS tier only emits error codes in `timeout|denied|io|invalid_request|internal`.
- [ ] Existing ext_conformance + event_loop determinism tests still pass.
- [ ] Logs remain deterministic and do not include raw params.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:34.065311493Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:34.065311493Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","hostcall","js"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:34.065311493Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:34.065311493Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
+{"id":"bd-1uy.1.4","title":"Hostcall ABI: Cross-Runtime Parity Tests (JS/WASM/Protocol)","description":"# Goal
Add tests that prove the hostcall ABI is stable and identical across:
- JS runtime hostcalls
- WASM hostcalls
- protocol adapter (`ExtensionMessage host_call -> host_result`)

# Why
Without parity tests, JS/WASM divergence will regress silently, undermining:
- security claims (policy enforcement)
- compatibility claims (extensions "just work")
- conformance harness reliability

# Scope
Create a small canonical suite of host_call examples (method+params) and assert:
1) All three paths return a `host_result` with:
   - the same `is_error` value
   - the same taxonomy error code (if error)
   - output object shape consistent with the method
2) Logs:
   - `host_call.start` and `host_call.end` exist
   - include `params_hash`
   - never include raw params

# Canonical Cases (Minimum)
- `tool`:
  - read-like (`grep`) success
  - unknown tool invalid_request
- `http`:
  - denied host -> denied
  - timeout -> timeout (use deterministic clock/harness)
- `exec`:
  - denied by policy -> denied
- `session`:
  - when session handle not configured -> denied or internal (taxonomy, deterministic)
- `ui`:
  - when UI handler not configured -> denied or timeout (taxonomy, deterministic)

# Implementation Notes
- Prefer table-driven tests.
- Avoid network flakiness: use local TCP listener like existing wasm-host tests.
- Ensure wasm-host tests run under `--features wasm-host`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tests fail if any runtime emits a non-taxonomy code.
- [ ] Tests fail if JS/WASM diverge for the same host_call.
- [ ] CI path includes both default and `wasm-host` feature test runs.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","hostcall","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-nom.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1v10","title":"Phase 0: Environment and Runtime Setup","description":"# Phase 0: Environment and Runtime Setup

## Purpose
Before we can do ANY differential testing, we need BOTH runtimes working and verified independently. This phase ensures:
1. The pi-mono TypeScript runtime can load and run extensions via Bun
2. The Rust QuickJS runtime can load and run extensions
3. A deterministic test environment exists (no randomness, fixed timestamps)

## Why This Is Phase 0
Everything else depends on this. If either runtime cannot load extensions, differential testing is impossible. We discovered Bun 1.3.8 is available at /home/ubuntu/.bun/bin/bun. The pi-mono source lives at legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/. We need to verify it builds and its extension loader works.

## Acceptance Criteria
- pi-mono extension loader.ts can load the hello.ts extension and produce output
- Rust QuickJS runtime can load the hello.ts extension and produce output
- Both outputs can be captured as structured JSON
- Deterministic mode eliminates timestamps, random values, cwd-dependent paths","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:14:07.671337047Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T17:36:15.918993495Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T17:36:15.918905561Z","close_reason":"Phase 0 complete: pi-mono loads extensions via Bun/jiti (tests/ext_conformance/ts_oracle/load_extension.ts), Rust QuickJS runtime loads extensions (tests/ext_conformance_diff.rs), both produce structured JSON, all 60 official extensions validated.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1v10","depends_on_id":"bd-3odv","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:14:07.724213001Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":309,"issue_id":"bd-1v10","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"KEY DISCOVERY: Bun 1.3.8 is available at /home/ubuntu/.bun/bin/bun, which also provides a node shim. pi-mono uses jiti for extension loading (not native Bun module resolution). The virtualModules mechanism in loader.ts maps package names to bundled versions (@sinclair/typebox, @mariozechner/pi-agent-core, etc.). In development mode it uses aliases instead. For our harness, we need to run in development mode (not Bun binary mode) so that jiti resolves imports via filesystem.","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:27:14Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1v8m","title":"Security suite: wasm import gating (PiWasm)","description":"# Goal
Ensure wasm executed via PiWasm cannot access OS without explicit connector imports.

# Scope
- Deny side-effectful imports without capability.
- Ensure traps/errors are deterministic and logged.

# Dependencies
- Blocked by PiWasm implementation under bd-1ry.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T03:15:12.498889128Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T03:15:12.505632917Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1v8m","depends_on_id":"bd-193","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T03:15:12.505607820Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1ve4","title":"CI: add cross-platform build/test matrix (linux/macOS/windows)","description":"# Goal
Increase release confidence by running CI on all target platforms (at minimum build + unit tests), and document any platform-specific exclusions.

# Background
The current CI workflow only runs on `ubuntu-latest`. That’s good for fast iteration but insufficient for shipping binaries.

# Scope
- Add a GitHub Actions matrix over:
  - `ubuntu-latest`
  - `macos-latest`
  - `windows-latest`
- For each OS:
  - `cargo fmt --check`
  - `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
  - `cargo test --all-targets`

# Platform Considerations
- System deps:
  - `fd` and `rg` are required/optional for tool tests; install equivalents per OS.
- Path dependencies:
  - CI currently clones sibling repos (asupersync/rich_rust/charmed_rust/sqlmodel_rust). Ensure the same approach works on macOS/Windows.
- VCR playback mode:
  - Keep `VCR_MODE=playback` for determinism.

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `ci.yml` runs on Linux/macOS/Windows.
- [ ] Any OS-specific skips are explicit and justified (no silent test omissions).
- [ ] Failures include enough logs to debug platform-specific issues.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss
[ ] Unit tests cover core success/failure + edge cases for this bead (add regression coverage if applicable)
[ ] Integration/E2E script exercises the end-to-end path (or explicitly marks N/A with rationale in notes) and emits detailed JSONL logs + artifacts per bd-4u9
[ ] Logs include inputs, outputs, timing, and redaction summary; artifacts list is deterministic
[ ] Any new fixtures/golden outputs are deterministic and documented
[ ] Quality gates pass: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test
[ ] Docs/fixtures updated if behavior or UX changes","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T21:21:48.739486913Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T19:27:15.998454113Z","closed_at":"2026-02-04T00:29:34.304318188Z","close_reason":"Cross-platform CI matrix in .github/workflows/ci.yml (commit 292102c)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1ve4","depends_on_id":"bd-gqtd","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T00:00:00Z","created_by":"import"}]}
@@ -324,6 +325,9 @@
 {"id":"bd-2u2s","title":"Static scan: identify valid extension entry points in corpus","description":"# Static scan: identify valid extension entry points in corpus

## Context
We have 1,167+ TS files in tests/ext_conformance/artifacts/. Not all are extension entry points. Some are utilities, configs, or sub-modules. We need to identify which files are actual extension entry points.

## What Makes a Valid Entry Point
From pi-mono loader.ts (legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/packages/coding-agent/src/core/extensions/loader.ts):
1. File must export a default function (the factory function)
2. The factory receives ExtensionAPI parameter
3. Common pattern: export default function(pi: ExtensionAPI) { ... }
4. Alternative: export default function init(api) { ... } (dynamic typing)
5. Multi-file extensions have index.ts or package.json with pi.extensions field

## What To Do
1. For each TS file, scan for: export default function, export default async function, export default (arrow function)
2. Check for ExtensionAPI type reference (strong signal) or pi.on/pi.registerTool/pi.events (weak signal)
3. Check directory structure: index.ts in a dir = likely entry point
4. Check package.json files for pi.extensions field
5. Output: JSON array of {path, confidence: high|medium|low, patterns_found: [...]}

## Key Patterns (from CompatibilityScanner in src/extensions.rs lines 127-414)
The Rust CompatibilityScanner already does similar work. We should match its patterns:
- ExtensionAPI import
- export default function
- registerTool calls
- pi.on / pi.events calls
- pi.session calls
- UI primitives (header, footer, overlay, widget, spinner)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every TS file in artifacts/ is scanned
- Output JSON classifies each as: entry_point, sub_module, non_extension, or unknown
- False positive rate < 5% (verified by spot-checking)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:18:10.397065595Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T07:36:15.024720761Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T07:36:15.024639710Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2u2s","depends_on_id":"bd-2dnl","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:18:10.449483570Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":316,"issue_id":"bd-2u2s","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Scanner complete: 1193 files → 305 entry points, 765 sub-modules, 117 non-extensions, 6 unknowns (0.5%). Tests: 3 passing. Output: docs/extension-entry-scan.json. Test file: tests/ext_entry_scan.rs. Patterns detected: export_default_function, ExtensionAPI_import, registerTool, registerCommand, registerProvider, event_hook, etc. Handles ExtensionFactory pattern, test files, .d.ts exclusion.","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:35:13Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2u8g","title":"Conformance: Standalone popular community extensions (10 exts)","description":"Conformance tests for standalone popular community extensions from various authors. Extensions (10): 1. pi-cost-dashboard (mrexodia) — Dashboard for API cost monitoring 2. pi-canvas (jyaunches) — Interactive TUI canvases (calendar, document, flights) 3. pi-notification-extension (lsj5031) — Telegram/bell alerts when agent finishes 4. pi-ssh-remote (cv) — Redirects all file operations to remote host via SSH 5. pi-dcp (zenobi-us) — Dynamic context pruning for intelligent optimization 6. pi-notify (ferologics) — Native desktop notifications via OSC 777 7. pi-ghostty-theme-sync (ogulcancelik) — Sync Ghostty terminal theme with pi session 8. pi-sketch (ogulcancelik) — Quick sketch pad, draw in browser, send to models 9. pi-screenshots-picker (Graffioh) — Screenshot picker for better selections 10. pi-super-curl (Graffioh) — Empowers curl with coding agent capabilities. Each from a different author, testing maximum diversity of extension patterns.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T06:19:15.370077254Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T01:38:10.987519180Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T01:38:10.987377807Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2u8g","depends_on_id":"bd-1oi7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T06:19:15.383078367Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2uh1","title":"Epic: Extension Discovery and Search — user-facing browsing, search, and extension info","description":"# Goal
Build the user-facing discovery layer so users can find, evaluate, and install extensions without needing to know exact package names or GitHub URLs ahead of time.

# Background
The package manager (src/package_manager.rs) already handles install/remove/update from npm, git, and local sources. The extension catalog (bd-3kp3) covers the internal manifest for pinning and corpus tracking. But there is NO user-facing discovery mechanism:
- Users cannot search for extensions by keyword
- Users cannot browse categories
- Users cannot see extension details before installing
- There is no quality signal (popularity, compat score, maintenance status)

This is the difference between "apt install package-name" (exists) and "apt search keyword" + "apt show package-name" (doesnt).

# Why This Matters
- Extension ecosystems live or die by discoverability — VSCode Marketplace, npm search, crates.io
- Without discovery, adoption is bottlenecked by word-of-mouth and documentation
- The conformance corpus already has 60+ validated extensions — users should be able to find them
- This is table stakes for any extension platform

# Scope
1. Registry protocol: how extension metadata is published and queried
2. CLI commands: pi search, pi info, pi list --available
3. Rich terminal rendering: extension READMEs, install counts, compat scores
4. Curated collections: categories and featured lists
5. Quality signals: conformance tier, last-updated, Pi version compatibility

# Out of Scope
- Hosting a central registry server (use GitHub API + npm registry as backends for now)
- Extension submission/review process (manual curation initially)
- Monetization or paid extensions

# Architecture
- Data source: GitHub API for repo metadata + npm registry for package metadata
- Local index: JSON cache of discovered extensions, refreshed periodically
- Search: Client-side fuzzy search over cached index (no server needed)
- Rendering: rich_rust tables for search results, glamour markdown for README rendering
- Categories: tags in extension manifest (pi.json or package.json pi field)

# Dependencies
- Depends on bd-3kp3 (Extension catalog, pinning, corpus manifest) for manifest schema
- Relates to bd-ofcj (Expand proven Pi extension coverage) for corpus to search over
- Relates to bd-34io (Classify & score candidates) for quality signals

# Children
1. Extension registry protocol spec + index schema
2. pi search CLI command
3. pi info CLI command (rich detail view)
4. Curated collections and category taxonomy
5. Quality signals computation and display","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:35:20.108245219Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:35:20.300592039Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cli","extensions","ux"]}
+{"id":"bd-2uh1.1","title":"Extension registry protocol spec and local index schema","description":"# Goal
Design the extension registry protocol — how extension metadata is fetched from remote sources (GitHub, npm), stored locally, and queried for search/browse operations.

# Background
The package manager (src/package_manager.rs) already knows how to install from npm and GitHub. But there is no structured index of AVAILABLE extensions. Users must know exact package names. This task designs the metadata layer that enables discovery.

# Deliverables

1. Remote data sources:
   - GitHub: Search GitHub repos with topic "pi-extension" via GitHub API
   - npm: Search npm registry for packages with "pi-extension" keyword
   - Curated list: static JSON manifest in a Pi-maintained repo (for quality-vetted extensions)

2. Local index schema (SQLite or JSON):
   {
     "version": 1,
     "last_refreshed": "2026-01-15T10:30:00Z",
     "extensions": [
       {
         "id": "auto-commit-on-exit",
         "name": "Auto Commit on Exit",
         "description": "Automatically commits changes when Pi session ends",
         "version": "1.2.0",
         "source": { "type": "npm", "package": "@user/pi-auto-commit" },
         "author": "username",
         "license": "MIT",
         "tags": ["git", "automation", "lifecycle"],
         "conformance_tier": 1,
         "pi_version_range": ">=0.5.0",
         "downloads_30d": 1500,
         "last_updated": "2026-01-10T00:00:00Z",
         "readme_url": "https://...",
         "repository_url": "https://..."
       }
     ]
   }

3. Refresh strategy:
   - On first "pi search": fetch and cache index
   - Auto-refresh if cache older than 24 hours
   - Manual refresh: "pi update-index"
   - Incremental: only fetch new/updated since last refresh

4. Search algorithm:
   - Fuzzy text match on name + description + tags
   - Weighted scoring: name match (3x) > tag match (2x) > description match (1x)
   - Sort by relevance * quality_signal (conformance tier, downloads)

# Files to Create/Modify
- New: src/extension_index.rs (index management, search, refresh)
- src/package_manager.rs: Wire index into install flow
- src/config.rs: Add index cache path

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Schema documented in docs/extension-registry.md
- [ ] Index refresh from GitHub + npm sources works
- [ ] Fuzzy search returns relevant results
- [ ] Cache invalidation after 24h

+{"id":"bd-2uh1.2","title":"pi search CLI command — search available extensions by keyword","description":"# Goal
Implement the "pi search <query>" CLI command that searches the extension index and displays results in a rich terminal table.

# Background
This is the primary user-facing discovery command. It queries the local extension index (built by the registry protocol spec task) and presents results in a scannable format.

# Usage
  pi search git          # Search for git-related extensions
  pi search "auto commit" # Phrase search
  pi search --tag provider # Filter by tag
  pi search --sort downloads # Sort by popularity
  pi search --limit 20     # Limit results

# Output Format (rich_rust table)
  Name              Version  Description                        Downloads  Tier
  auto-commit       1.2.0    Auto commit on session exit         1,500     T1
  git-branch-hook   0.5.0    Switch branches via /git command      890     T2
  git-stats         2.0.1    Show git statistics in session        650     T1

  3 extensions found. Install with: pi install <name>

# Implementation Plan
1. Add "search" subcommand to clap CLI (src/cli.rs)
2. In main.rs, route to search handler
3. Search handler:
   a. Load or refresh extension index
   b. Apply fuzzy search with query
   c. Apply filters (--tag, --tier, --source)
   d. Sort results (--sort: relevance (default), downloads, name, updated)
   e. Render rich_rust table with columns: Name, Version, Description, Downloads, Tier
   f. Print install hint at bottom

4. Auto-refresh:
   - If index doesnt exist or is >24h old, refresh before searching
   - Show "Refreshing extension index..." message during refresh
   - Cache refreshed index for subsequent searches

# Files to Modify
- src/cli.rs: Add search subcommand with args
- src/main.rs: Route search subcommand
- New: src/extension_search.rs or add to extension_index.rs

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] "pi search git" returns relevant results
- [ ] Results sorted by relevance by default
- [ ] --tag filter works
- [ ] --sort flag works (relevance, downloads, name, updated)
- [ ] Rich table output with colors
- [ ] Install hint shown at bottom
- [ ] Auto-refresh of stale index
- [ ] Graceful error on network failure (show cached results if available)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:49.290045657Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:49.290045657Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cli","discovery","extensions"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:49.290045657Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2uhs","title":"Task: Implement Exec Hostcall Handler (pi.exec)","description":"# Task: Implement Exec Hostcall Handler

## Objective

Implement the handler for HostcallKind::Exec that routes pi.exec() calls to shell command execution.

## Background

When an extension calls pi.exec("git", ["status"], {cwd: "/path"}), it should execute the command and return stdout/stderr/exitCode. This is similar to the bash tool but with more granular control.

## TypeScript Reference (loader.ts:215-216)

```typescript
exec(command: string, args: string[], options?: ExecOptions) {
    return execCommand(command, args, options?.cwd ?? cwd, options);
}
```

## Implementation

```rust
impl ExtensionDispatcher {
    /// Handle pi.exec(command, args, options) hostcalls.
    /// 
    /// Executes a shell command with optional working directory and timeout.
    /// Unlike the bash tool, this provides direct command execution without
    /// shell interpretation (no pipes, redirects, etc. unless explicitly using sh -c).
    async fn dispatch_exec(
        &self,
        cmd: &str,
        args: &[String],
        opts: ExecOptions,
    ) -> HostcallOutcome {
        use std::process::Stdio;
        use asupersync::Command;
        
        let cwd = opts.cwd
            .map(PathBuf::from)
            .unwrap_or_else(|| self.cwd.clone());
        
        let timeout = opts.timeout_ms.unwrap_or(120_000); // Default 2 min
        
        // Build command
        let mut command = Command::new(cmd);
        command
            .args(args)
            .current_dir(&cwd)
            .stdin(Stdio::null())
            .stdout(Stdio::piped())
            .stderr(Stdio::piped());
        
        // Set environment if provided
        if let Some(env) = opts.env {
            for (k, v) in env {
                command.env(k, v);
            }
        }
        
        // Execute with timeout
        let result = match tokio::time::timeout(
            Duration::from_millis(timeout),
            command.output(),
        ).await {
            Ok(Ok(output)) => {
                let stdout = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout).to_string();
                let stderr = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr).to_string();
                let exit_code = output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1);
                
                HostcallOutcome::Success(serde_json::json!({
                    "stdout": stdout,
                    "stderr": stderr,
                    "exitCode": exit_code,
                    "signal": output.status.signal(),
                }))
            }
            Ok(Err(e)) => HostcallOutcome::Error {
                code: "EXEC_ERROR".to_string(),
                message: e.to_string(),
            },
            Err(_) => HostcallOutcome::Error {
                code: "TIMEOUT".to_string(),
                message: format!("Command timed out after {}ms", timeout),
            },
        };
        
        result
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default)]
pub struct ExecOptions {
    pub cwd: Option<String>,
    pub timeout_ms: Option<u64>,
    pub env: Option<HashMap<String, String>>,
}
```

## Security Considerations

1. **Path Traversal**: Validate cwd is within allowed directories
2. **Command Injection**: Args are passed directly, not through shell
3. **Resource Limits**: Enforce timeout, consider memory limits
4. **Capability Check**: Requires "exec" capability in extension manifest

## Differences from Bash Tool

| Aspect | pi.exec() | bash tool |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Shell interpretation | None (direct exec) | Full shell |
| Arguments | Array of args | Single command string |
| Pipes/redirects | Not supported | Supported |
| Use case | Precise commands | Complex scripts |

## Testing

1. Unit test: Simple command (echo)
2. Unit test: Command with args
3. Unit test: Custom cwd
4. Unit test: Timeout handling
5. Unit test: Non-zero exit code
6. Integration test: Real command execution

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bd-389q (ExtensionDispatcher struct)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] dispatch_exec() method implemented
- [ ] Command execution with args works
- [ ] Custom cwd respected
- [ ] Timeout handling works
- [ ] stdout/stderr/exitCode returned
- [ ] Unit tests pass","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CoralBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:53:09.935871275Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T21:06:49.420847034Z","closed_at":"2026-02-04T21:06:49.420776342Z","close_reason":"Implemented HostcallKind::Exec dispatch in ExtensionDispatcher (Command spawn + timeout/kill) with unix-only tests for success + command-not-found; gates green","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uhs","depends_on_id":"bd-37qz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:53:09.944944704Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uhs","depends_on_id":"bd-389q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:53:16.488550732Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

+{"id":"bd-nom.1","title":"Hostcall ABI: WASM host.call Uses Shared Dispatcher (Parity Refactor)","description":"# Goal
Refactor the WASM component hostcall path (`docs/wit/extension.wit` -> `host.call(...)`) so it uses the **shared HostCallPayload -> HostResultPayload dispatcher**.

# Background
- Current WASM hostcall implementation lives in `src/extensions.rs` under `mod wasm_host`, specifically `HostState::call()`.
- It already enforces policy + timeouts + logging, but it is bespoke code.
- We need **JS/WASM parity** at the ABI level and a single enforcement point.

# Scope
- Replace method dispatch + policy/timeout/error mapping inside `HostState::call()` with:
  - parse JSON -> `extensions::HostCallPayload`
  - call shared dispatcher
  - convert `HostResultPayload` to the WIT return type:
    - success: JSON string output
    - error: JSON string `HostCallError` (taxonomy codes only)

# Required Semantics
- Preserve WIT contract: `host.call(name, input_json) -> result<string, string>`.
- Enforce `name == payload.method` (or keep as validation step).
- Ensure logs remain identical in shape to JS for equivalent calls.

# Tests
- Update/add `wasm-host` feature tests:
  - capability mismatch -> invalid_request
  - denied -> denied
  - timeout -> timeout
  - parity snapshot tests vs JS for a small set of canonical calls (tool/http/session/ui).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No duplicated policy/timeout/error mapping logic remains in WASM hostcall path.
- [ ] WASM hostcall errors map to the same taxonomy as JS.
- [ ] `cargo test --all-targets --features wasm-host` remains green.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:36.997165900Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:36.997165900Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","hostcall","wasm"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-nom.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:36.997165900Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-nom.1","depends_on_id":"bd-nom","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:36.997165900Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-np43","title":"Task: Integrate extension commands into interactive processor","description":"# Task: Integrate Extension Commands into Interactive Processor

## Objective

Modify the interactive command processor to recognize and route extension-registered slash commands.

## Background

The interactive module processes user input, including slash commands. Extension commands must be integrated:
1. When user types /foo, check if foo is an extension command
2. If so, route to extension handler instead of built-in processing
3. Handle the result and display output

## Implementation

### Add Extension Command Check

```rust
async fn process_command(&mut self, input: &str) -> Result<CommandAction> {
    let (cmd, args) = parse_command(input);
    
    // Check built-in commands first (they take precedence)
    match cmd {
        "help" => return self.show_help(),
        "clear" => return self.clear_history(),
        "model" => return self.change_model(args),
        _ => {}
    }
    
    // Check extension commands
    if let Some(runtime) = &self.extension_runtime {
        let ext_commands = runtime.get_registered_commands().await?;
        
        let is_ext_cmd = ext_commands.iter().any(|c| {
            c.name == cmd || c.aliases.contains(&cmd.to_string())
        });
        
        if is_ext_cmd {
            return self.execute_extension_command(cmd, args).await;
        }
    }
    
    Err(anyhow!("Unknown command: /{}", cmd))
}
```

### Execute Extension Command

```rust
async fn execute_extension_command(&mut self, name: &str, args: &str) -> Result<CommandAction> {
    let runtime = self.extension_runtime.as_ref()
        .ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("No extension runtime"))?;
    
    let result = runtime.execute_command(name, args).await?;
    
    // Display output if any
    if let Some(output) = result.output {
        self.display_output(&output)?;
    }
    
    // Append messages if any
    if let Some(messages) = result.messages {
        for msg in messages {
            self.conversation.push(msg);
        }
    }
    
    if result.continue_loop { Ok(CommandAction::Continue) } else { Ok(CommandAction::Exit) }
}
```

### Update Help Command

```rust
async fn show_help(&self) -> Result<CommandAction> {
    println!("Built-in commands:");
    println!("  /help    - Show this help");
    println!("  /clear   - Clear conversation");
    
    if let Some(runtime) = &self.extension_runtime {
        let ext_commands = runtime.get_registered_commands().await?;
        
        if !ext_commands.is_empty() {
            println!("\nExtension commands:");
            for cmd in ext_commands {
                let usage = cmd.usage.as_deref().unwrap_or("");
                println!("  /{:<8} - {} {}", cmd.name, cmd.description, usage);
            }
        }
    }
    
    Ok(CommandAction::Continue)
}
```

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (7 cases)
1. test_builtin_commands_still_work
2. test_extension_command_recognized
3. test_extension_command_executed_via_js
4. test_output_displayed_correctly
5. test_unknown_command_returns_error
6. test_builtin_commands_take_precedence
7. test_extension_aliases_work

### E2E Test Script (tests/e2e/interactive_commands.sh)

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

LOG_FILE="artifacts/interactive_commands_e2e.jsonl"
mkdir -p artifacts

log() {
    echo "{\"timestamp\":\"$(date -Iseconds)\",\"event\":\"$1\",\"data\":$2}" >> "$LOG_FILE"
}

log "test_start" '{"suite":"interactive_commands"}'

# Create test extension
mkdir -p /tmp/test_interactive_ext
cat > /tmp/test_interactive_ext/index.js << 'EOF'
export function activate(ctx) {
    ctx.registerCommand({
        name: "echo",
        description: "Echo arguments back",
        aliases: ["e"],
        handler: async (args) => ({ output: args || "(empty)" }),
    });
}
EOF

# Test with tmux for interactive mode
if command -v tmux &> /dev/null; then
    SESSION="pi_test_$$"
    tmux new-session -d -s "$SESSION" "pi --extension /tmp/test_interactive_ext"
    sleep 2
    
    # Type /help and capture
    tmux send-keys -t "$SESSION" "/help" Enter
    sleep 1
    HELP_OUTPUT=$(tmux capture-pane -t "$SESSION" -p)
    log "help_output" "{\"contains_echo\":$(echo "$HELP_OUTPUT" | grep -q "echo" && echo true || echo false)}"
    
    # Type /echo test
    tmux send-keys -t "$SESSION" "/echo hello world" Enter
    sleep 1
    ECHO_OUTPUT=$(tmux capture-pane -t "$SESSION" -p)
    log "echo_output" "{\"output\":\"$ECHO_OUTPUT\"}"
    
    # Type /e (alias)
    tmux send-keys -t "$SESSION" "/e alias test" Enter
    sleep 1
    
    # Cleanup
    tmux send-keys -t "$SESSION" "/quit" Enter
    sleep 1
    tmux kill-session -t "$SESSION" 2>/dev/null || true
    
    log "test_end" '{"status":"pass"}'
else
    log "test_skip" '{"reason":"tmux not available"}'
fi

echo "Interactive commands E2E test complete. Logs: $LOG_FILE"
```

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bd-ie1g (execute_command)
- Depends on: bd-2qrn (get_registered_commands)
- Part of: bd-20vx (Slash Command Handler feature)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Built-in commands take precedence
- [ ] Extension commands appear in /help
- [ ] Results displayed correctly
- [ ] Aliases work
- [ ] 7 unit tests pass

 {"id":"bd-ocv","title":"Unit tests: error type conversions + display invariants","description":"# Goal
Cover `src/error.rs` with unit tests for constructors, From conversions, and user-facing Display messages.

# Scope / Deliverables
- `Error::config/session/provider/auth/tool/validation/extension/api` constructors.
- `From<std::io::Error>`, `From<serde_json::Error>`, `From<sqlmodel_core::Error>`, `From<asupersync::sync::LockError>` mappings.
- Display formatting includes context (provider/tool names, paths).
- Ensure errors are stable for snapshot/log comparisons.

# No‑Mock Rule
- Use real error types and real error values (temp files, malformed JSON, sqlite errors).

 {"id":"bd-ofcj","title":"Expand proven Pi extension coverage (research→conformance)","description":"# Goal
Dramatically expand the set of Pi extensions that are **proven to work unmodified** across all major extension shapes (skills, prompts, tools, MCP servers, providers, templates, packages).

# Outcome
A curated, version‑pinned catalog with conformance + performance gates that prove compatibility, reliability, and speed.

# Scope
Research → selection → acquisition → conformance → performance → documentation → refresh workflow.

# Non‑Goals
Do not modify third‑party extension source code; we only vendor exact upstream artifacts and validate them.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:18:34.843067957Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T08:18:30.519275035Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":317,"issue_id":"bd-ofcj","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Background: The Pi extension ecosystem is spread across npm, GitHub, and template/skill packs, and current conformance coverage only proves a limited subset.

Reasoning: Proving that popular extensions run unmodified reduces maintenance and increases user trust; this requires a full pipeline from research to testing to publication.

Considerations: Artifacts must remain byte-for-byte upstream, version-pinned, and validated with correctness + performance + reliability gates.

Exit criteria: A curated catalog and test reports exist, all selected extensions pass or are explicitly excluded with rationale.","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:44:52Z"},{"id":386,"issue_id":"bd-ofcj","author":"LavenderRobin","text":"SCALE TARGET (USER REQUIREMENT)

Given the ecosystem size (OpenClaw/marketplaces + GitHub/npm), the “proven unmodified” set must be large enough to be convincing.

Target
- Tier-1 corpus: >= 200 extensions (true Pi extensions, unmodified)
- Tier-0 baseline: official pi-mono examples remain must-pass

Evidence requirements
- Deterministic conformance + perf/reliability gates with JSONL logs + diff artifacts.
- Offline/fixture mode in CI; live refresh is opt-in.
